teaching_arc.entry_point: Welcome students into the first robotics lab by explaining what this course will actually feel like: simulation-first manipulator work, not a disconnected list of tools.
teaching_arc.progression: Start with the course direction, explain why simulation is the safe and practical entry point for robot arms, set expectations for workload and setup, then preview the ROS 2, RViz2, Gazebo, MoveIt, IK, planning, and debugging stack.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should leave Session 1 knowing the course path, what must be installed before Session 2, and why the software stack matters for manipulator projects.

audience_level: undergraduate robotics lab students who may know basic programming but are new to simulation-first robot manipulator workflows with Ubuntu, ROS 2, and MoveIt
topic_goal: Orient students to the robotics lab, establish the simulation-first manipulator workflow, and make the Ubuntu 22.04 preparation for Session 2 concrete.
topic_takeaways: Students should understand that this course is about robot manipulators in simulation, not mobile robot navigation.
Students should know the main stack: Ubuntu 22.04, ROS 2 Humble, RViz2, rqt, Gazebo, robot descriptions, TF, MoveIt, inverse kinematics, and motion planning.
Students should know exactly what laptop setup must be ready before Session 2.
Students should feel that setup work is part of engineering practice, not a side task.
style_notes: Open like a real first class: welcome students, explain the direction, and give them confidence that the tools will connect.
Do not read installation slides word for word; summarize the safe rule, what to verify, and when to pause before changing disks.
Keep connecting setup decisions back to the future manipulator workflow so students understand why the details matter.
Use the final slides as a clear handoff: next session is not installation day; Ubuntu must already be ready.
transition_style: Move from course purpose to practical setup, then from setup to the manipulator software stack, always reminding students how the current step prepares the next lab session.

slides.narration_seed: Welcome everyone. This is Session 1 of the robotics lab.
slides.transition_from_previous: Open by framing the topic goal: Help students understand course overview in a way they can explain, apply, and build on later.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into COURSE OVERVIEW + WHAT CHANGED so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Frame ROBOTICS LAB - SESSION 1 as the question or capability the rest of the topic is going to answer.
slides.story_hint: Keep ROBOTICS LAB - SESSION 1 connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how ROBOTICS LAB - SESSION 1 sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: This is the first framing shift: we are not building a TurtleBot navigation course here. We are using the same strong ROS foundation, but the robot is now a manipulator.
must_cover: This lab is about robot manipulators, not mobile robots
We will work completely in simulation
We will use 6-DOF and 7-DOF robot arms
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into WHY SIMULATION FOR MANIPULATORS? so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
prop_suggestions: MoveIt Documentation - MoveIt Quickstart in RViz
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for COURSE OVERVIEW + WHAT CHANGED first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep COURSE OVERVIEW + WHAT CHANGED connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture COURSE OVERVIEW + WHAT CHANGED clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Manipulator simulation is not just a convenience. It lets us test plans quickly, make mistakes safely, and inspect the system before anything physical is at risk.
must_cover: Faster iteration: test, reset, repeat
Safer for learning IK, collisions, and planning
Better for debugging robot state, frames, and trajectories
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into WHAT YOU WILL LEARN so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture WHY SIMULATION FOR MANIPULATORS? clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: This is the learning stack for the course. The tools are not isolated: URDF gives the robot structure, TF connects frames, RViz2 helps us see the state, MoveIt gives us planning and IK tools, and Gazebo provides simulation.
must_cover: ROS 2, RViz2, Gazebo, rqt, and MoveIt
URDF/Xacro, robot frames, and visualization
Inverse kinematics and motion planning

slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into HOW THE LAB WILL RUN so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: The lab sequence is cumulative. Installation and ROS fundamentals are not separate from the project; they are the foundation for everything later.
must_cover: We start with installation and ROS 2 fundamentals
Then we build toward manipulators, IK, and planning
Most project integration happens in the final sessions

slides.narration_seed: Project 1 is guided, but it is not small. It is closer to the work of two smaller assignments.
must_cover: Project 1 is a larger guided assignment
Think of Project 1 as the work of two smaller assignments
Project 2 is more like a project: integration and demonstration

must_cover: Ubuntu 22.04 LTS
ROS 2 Humble
RViz2 + rqt

slides.narration_seed: Simulation can be demanding, especially when we run GUI tools and planning components. Sixteen gigabytes of RAM is the comfortable recommendation.
must_cover: 16 GB RAM is recommended
8 GB RAM can work, but simulation may feel slow
Aim for 80-120 GB free if possible
slides.transition_from_previous: Bridge from SOFTWARE STACK WE WILL USE into LAPTOP EXPECTATIONS so the lesson feels like one continuous explanation rather than isolated slides.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into WHY UBUNTU 22.04 + ROS 2 HUMBLE so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.story_hint: Keep LAPTOP EXPECTATIONS connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how LAPTOP EXPECTATIONS sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slides.narration_seed: The version baseline is intentional. ROS 2 Humble is the ROS distribution we will use, and Ubuntu 22.04 is the operating system baseline.
must_cover: This course is standardized on Ubuntu 22.04 + ROS 2 Humble
Use exactly these versions unless I tell you otherwise
Consistent versions reduce package and driver problems
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into UBUNTU INSTALL OPTIONS so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.

slides.narration_seed: Dual boot gives the best performance for simulation, which is why it is the best option for this course. A virtual machine can work for lighter command-line practice, but heavier simulation and graphics can become frustrating.
must_cover: Best for this course: dual boot with Ubuntu 22.04
VM is acceptable for light work, but not ideal for heavier simulation
Ubuntu-only is also fine if you already chose that path

Option 2: use these slides as the full step-by-step guide

Use an 8 GB or larger USB drive
Download Ubuntu 22.04 LTS Desktop ISO

slide_ref.source_title: WINDOWS STEP 1 - TURN OFF FAST STARTUP

slides.narration_seed: Fast Startup can leave the Windows filesystem in a state that causes problems for dual boot. Turn it off before shrinking the disk or booting the installer.
must_cover: Open Control Panel
Go to Power Options
Click Choose what the power buttons do
Click Change settings that are currently unavailable

slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.

slides.explanation_style: Keep WINDOWS STEP 1 - TURN OFF FAST STARTUP efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on WINDOWS STEP 1 - TURN OFF FAST STARTUP and what to verify before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Disk Management is where students create free space for Ubuntu. They should identify the main Windows disk carefully before shrinking anything.
must_cover: Right-click the Windows Start button
Click Disk Management
Find your main Windows drive, usually C:
Look at the bottom disk view, not only the top list
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.

slides.narration_seed: The goal is to create unallocated space for Ubuntu. Students should leave it unallocated.
must_cover: Right-click C: and choose Shrink Volume...
Enter the amount to shrink
Recommended range: 60000-120000 MB
Click Shrink

slides.narration_seed: The most common mistake at this step is selecting the wrong USB device. Students must confirm the device before starting.
must_cover: Plug in your USB drive
Open Rufus
Confirm the correct Device is selected
Warning: Rufus will erase the selected USB drive

slide_ref.source_title: RUFUS STEP 2 - SELECT THE UBUNTU ISO

slides.narration_seed: Students should use Ubuntu 22.04 LTS Desktop, not a newer release and not a server image.
must_cover: Click SELECT
Choose the downloaded Ubuntu 22.04 LTS Desktop ISO
Rufus will auto-detect most settings
Do not use a different Ubuntu release for this course

slides.narration_seed: Most current student laptops use UEFI, so GPT is usually correct. If a student has an older legacy BIOS system, that may be different, but GPT is the default assumption for the course.
must_cover: For most modern laptops, choose GPT
Target system should be UEFI
Older legacy BIOS systems may need MBR
If you are unsure, most students should choose GPT
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on RUFUS STEP 3 - CHOOSE PARTITION SCHEME and what to verify before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: The exact boot menu key depends on the laptop brand. If students see multiple USB entries, the UEFI entry is usually the right one.
must_cover: Restart the computer with the USB plugged in
Open the boot menu during startup
Common keys: F12, ESC, F9, or F10

slides.narration_seed: Trying Ubuntu first gives students a quick hardware check before changing the disk. If Wi-Fi or graphics are badly broken, it is better to notice before installation.
must_cover: Choose Try Ubuntu first if available
Check mouse, keyboard, display, and Wi-Fi
Then start Install Ubuntu 22.04 LTS

Choose the correct keyboard layout
Test the keyboard in the text box

slide_ref.source_title: INSTALLER STEP 2 - UPDATES AND THIRD-PARTY SOFTWARE

slides.narration_seed: For a robotics laptop, graphics and Wi-Fi matter. The third-party software option can help with hardware support, especially on laptops with proprietary drivers.
must_cover: Choose Normal installation
Check Download updates while installing Ubuntu
Check Install third-party software if available
This can help graphics and Wi-Fi hardware

slides.narration_seed: This is the screen where a wrong choice can erase Windows. Students should slow down here.
must_cover: Read this screen carefully
Best easy path: Install Ubuntu alongside Windows Boot Manager
Manual path: Something else
Do NOT choose Erase disk unless you want to remove Windows

slides.narration_seed: If you reserved around 80 to 120 GB for Ubuntu, my recommendation is to give about 45 to 60 GB to / and give the rest to /home if you want a separate home partition. If you just want one / partition, that is also fully acceptable for this course.
must_cover: Simplest acceptable setup: one root partition /
Optional long-term setup: / + /home + optional swap
If Ubuntu space is 80-120 GB, use roughly:

slides.narration_seed: Manual partitioning is only safe if students can identify the free space they created. Windows partitions should be left alone.
must_cover: Look for Free space in the partition list
This should match the unallocated space made in Windows
Do NOT select Windows partitions
Create Ubuntu partitions only inside the free space
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.

slides.narration_seed: The simplest acceptable setup is one root partition. It is not the most flexible long-term layout, but it is fully acceptable for this course and avoids unnecessary install complexity.
must_cover: Select Free space and click +
Use most or all of the Ubuntu space
Use as: Ext4 journaling file system
Mount point: /
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.

slides.narration_seed: If students want a separate home partition, first create root. For this course, around 45 to 60 GB is a reasonable root size when total Ubuntu space is 80 to 120 GB.
must_cover: Select Free space and click +
Size: 45-60 GB if you reserved 80-120 GB total
Use as: Ext4 journaling file system
Mount point: /

slides.narration_seed: A separate home partition is useful because it separates personal files from the root system partition, but it is not required for this course.
must_cover: Select remaining Free space and click +
Use most of the remaining space
Use as: Ext4 journaling file system
Mount point: /home
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.

slides.narration_seed: Swap is optional. Students can use a simple root-only setup, or root plus home, without creating a swap partition.
must_cover: Swap is extra memory on disk when RAM is full
It is slower than RAM
If you want swap, select remaining free space and click +
Use as: swap area
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.

slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on OPTIONAL - CREATE SWAP AREA and what to verify before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: EFI PARTITION - DO NOT FORMAT WINDOWS EFI

slides.narration_seed: This is an important warning. On most dual-boot systems, Windows already has an EFI partition.
must_cover: Most Windows UEFI systems already have an EFI System Partition
It is usually a small FAT32 partition
Do not format it
slides.transition_from_previous: Use EFI PARTITION - DO NOT FORMAT WINDOWS EFI to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once EFI PARTITION - DO NOT FORMAT WINDOWS EFI is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define EFI PARTITION - DO NOT FORMAT WINDOWS EFI in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep EFI PARTITION - DO NOT FORMAT WINDOWS EFI connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain EFI PARTITION - DO NOT FORMAT WINDOWS EFI accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: The bootloader should go to the main disk, not a specific Ubuntu partition. Device names vary, so students should be careful and ask if unsure.
must_cover: Find Device for boot loader installation
Choose the main disk
Examples: /dev/nvme0n1 or /dev/sda
slides.transition_from_previous: Use BOOTLOADER LOCATION to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once BOOTLOADER LOCATION is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define BOOTLOADER LOCATION in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep BOOTLOADER LOCATION connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain BOOTLOADER LOCATION accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: CONFIRM CHANGES BEFORE WRITING TO DISK

slides.narration_seed: This is the last checkpoint before the installer writes changes to disk. Students should verify the layout before continuing.
must_cover: Check that Ubuntu partitions are inside the free space
Check that Windows partitions are untouched
Check that / is present
If using /home, check that it is present
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.

slides.narration_seed: The timezone step is straightforward, but it confirms the installer is now past the dangerous partitioning stage.
must_cover: Select your city or timezone
Use the map or search box
Click Continue
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.

slides.narration_seed: Students must remember their Ubuntu password. They will need it whenever they install packages or run administrative commands.
must_cover: Enter your name
Enter a computer name
Choose a username
Choose a password you will remember
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.

slides.narration_seed: After installation, students should remove the USB so the computer boots from the internal disk, not the installer again.
must_cover: When prompted, click Restart Now
Remove the USB when the installer asks
If it shuts down first, remove the USB before booting again
You should see a boot menu with Ubuntu and Windows
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.
likely_student_confusion: Students may copy steps mechanically and miss the verification point that tells them the step actually worked.

slides.narration_seed: For next session, I do not want to spend the first part of lab waiting for Ubuntu installs to finish. Ubuntu 22.04 must already be installed, booting, updated, and ready.
must_cover: Connect to Wi-Fi
Open Terminal
Run: sudo apt update
Run: sudo apt upgrade -y

slides.narration_seed: This is useful if Wi-Fi or graphics are not working well. Not every machine will show additional drivers, and that is okay.
must_cover: Open Software & Updates
Go to Additional Drivers
Select the recommended driver if one is available
Apply changes

slide_ref.source_title: WHAT MUST BE READY FOR NEXT SESSION

slides.narration_seed: Make the expectation concrete. Next session is not installation day; Ubuntu should already be ready so the class can move into ROS setup and robotics tools.
must_cover: Ubuntu 22.04 installed and booting
You can log in
You can connect to Wi-Fi
slides.transition_from_previous: Use WHAT MUST BE READY FOR NEXT SESSION to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once WHAT MUST BE READY FOR NEXT SESSION is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define WHAT MUST BE READY FOR NEXT SESSION in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep WHAT MUST BE READY FOR NEXT SESSION connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain WHAT MUST BE READY FOR NEXT SESSION accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: ROS 2 is the layer that lets different parts of a robot system communicate and be inspected. For this lab, it is the foundation that connects robot descriptions, state publishers, visualization, planning, and control.
must_cover: ROS 2 is the middleware and tooling layer for robot systems
It connects robot state, sensors, planning, and control
We will use it as the foundation for the whole lab
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into ROS 2 CORE CONCEPTS so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for WHAT IS ROS 2? first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep WHAT IS ROS 2? connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture WHAT IS ROS 2? clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: The first ROS debugging habit is to look at the graph. If students cannot see which nodes exist and which topics connect them, they will guess.
must_cover: You will use nodes, topics, services, actions, and parameters
Most debugging starts with the graph and the topic data
Open rqt_graph early instead of guessing
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into PACKAGES, WORKSPACES, COLCON, AND LAUNCH FILES so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for ROS 2 CORE CONCEPTS first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep ROS 2 CORE CONCEPTS connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture ROS 2 CORE CONCEPTS clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slide_ref.source_title: PACKAGES, WORKSPACES, COLCON, AND LAUNCH FILES

slides.narration_seed: ROS 2 work is organized around packages inside workspaces. We build with colcon, and we use launch files so that complete systems start consistently instead of relying on several manual terminal commands.
must_cover: Package = one reusable ROS 2 unit
Workspace = where your packages live
colcon = how we build our workspace
launch files = how we start complete systems cleanly
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.
likely_student_confusion: Students may copy steps mechanically and miss the verification point that tells them the step actually worked.
slides.explanation_style: Keep PACKAGES, WORKSPACES, COLCON, AND LAUNCH FILES efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on PACKAGES, WORKSPACES, COLCON, AND LAUNCH FILES and what to verify before moving on.
slides.scene_hint: Use a lightweight node-and-topic diagram only long enough to show the system relationships behind the command sequence.

slides.narration_seed: RViz2 is not the simulator. It is the visualization and inspection tool.
must_cover: RViz2 is where you inspect robot state, markers, and trajectories
It helps you answer: "Is the data correct?"
It helps you answer: "Is the robot doing what I think it is doing?"
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into GAZEBO FOR THIS LAB so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for RVIZ2 first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture RVIZ2 clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Show the robot model, TF, and RViz displays as a simple pipeline so students can connect each layer to the visual outcome.

slides.narration_seed: I am saying Gazebo on the slide, not Gazebo Classic. Follow my tested guide for the exact packages.
must_cover: Gazebo is our main simulation environment
We will use simulation to test behavior before project integration
Follow the tested course guide for the exact Gazebo setup
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into ROBOT DESCRIPTION, URDF/XACRO, AND TF so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for GAZEBO FOR THIS LAB first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep GAZEBO FOR THIS LAB connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture GAZEBO FOR THIS LAB clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Before planning anything, the robot must be described correctly. Links, joints, and frames define the robot structure.
must_cover: A manipulator starts with links, joints, and frames
URDF describes the robot structure
Xacro keeps the model clean and reusable
must_say: Links are rigid bodies, and joints define how those bodies connect or move.
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into MOVEIT so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think URDF, Xacro, links, and joints are just file syntax instead of a robot-structure model.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for ROBOT DESCRIPTION, URDF/XACRO, AND TF first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Tell it like a debugging moment where the robot is not showing up correctly and students need the right mental model to fix it.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture ROBOT DESCRIPTION, URDF/XACRO, AND TF clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use a TurtleBot or small car with attached coordinate frames so students can see relative transforms instead of imagining them abstractly.

slides.narration_seed: MoveIt is the main manipulation framework for this course. It is where inverse kinematics, planning scenes, collision checking, and motion planning come together.
must_cover: MoveIt is the manipulation framework we will use
It gives us IK, planning scenes, collision checking, and motion planning
We will use it in RViz2 and inside our projects
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into INVERSE KINEMATICS so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for MOVEIT first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep MOVEIT connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture MOVEIT clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Inverse kinematics starts with the desired end-effector pose and asks what joint values can reach it. For 6-DOF and 7-DOF arms, the solution may not be unique, and with redundancy we get extra freedom that can be useful or confusing if we do not debug carefully.
must_cover: IK asks: what joint values place the end-effector at a target pose?
For 6-DOF and 7-DOF arms, more than one valid solution may exist
In 7-DOF arms, redundancy gives extra freedom
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into MOTION PLANNING so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for INVERSE KINEMATICS first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep INVERSE KINEMATICS connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture INVERSE KINEMATICS clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Planning is more than finding a final pose. The path itself must be valid.
must_cover: Motion planning is not only "reach the goal"
It is "reach the goal with a valid, collision-free trajectory"
Planning scenes and obstacles matter
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into RQT AND DEBUGGING WORKFLOW so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for MOTION PLANNING first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep MOTION PLANNING connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture MOTION PLANNING clearly before the formal details continue.
slides.scene_hint: Use the visual or interactive asset on this slide as the main anchor, then return attention to the slide summary before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: The debugging order matters. Start with topics so you know what data exists.
must_cover: Use rqt_graph to inspect the system graph
Use RViz2 to inspect frames, robot state, and trajectories
Debug first: topics
slides.transition_from_previous: Pause the formal explanation and build a picture students can carry into the next technical slide.
slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into WORK OUTSIDE CLASS so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
slides.explanation_style: Build a picture for RQT AND DEBUGGING WORKFLOW first, then connect that picture back to the formal meaning students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Keep RQT AND DEBUGGING WORKFLOW connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to picture RQT AND DEBUGGING WORKFLOW clearly before the formal details continue.

slides.narration_seed: The class time is limited. Some setup and practice has to happen outside class, especially installation and repeated tool practice.
must_cover: We only have about 6 lab sessions
Some setup and practice must be done at home
I will provide a tested step-by-step guide
slides.transition_from_previous: Use WORK OUTSIDE CLASS to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once WORK OUTSIDE CLASS is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define WORK OUTSIDE CLASS in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep WORK OUTSIDE CLASS connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain WORK OUTSIDE CLASS accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slides.narration_seed: Installation problems are often machine-specific. BitLocker, Intel RST or AHCI settings, Fast Startup, and partition choices are common blockers.
must_cover: First try to solve machine-specific issues on your own
Common blockers: BitLocker, Intel RST/AHCI, Fast Startup, wrong partition choice
Search the exact error before changing random settings
If you are still stuck, email me: Ali.Karimzade@uOttawa.ca
slides.transition_from_previous: After the concept slide, move into the concrete workflow or command sequence students will actually run.
slides.transition_to_next: After the procedure, remind students what successful output or behavior they should expect next.

slides.explanation_style: Keep IF INSTALLATION GOES WRONG efficient and procedural, highlighting only the information students need to act or debug correctly.
slides.story_hint: Frame the slide as a lab workflow students will run themselves, including what they should verify before moving on.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know the minimum reliable workflow on IF INSTALLATION GOES WRONG and what to verify before moving on.

slides.narration_seed: Close like a real first lab: thank students, make the next-session expectation concrete, and remind them that setup is what lets us spend class time on robotics instead of installation.
must_cover: For next session, Ubuntu 22.04 must be ready
Bring a working install, your charger, and your notes
Do not wait until project time to start putting things together
slides.transition_from_previous: Use FINAL REMINDER to consolidate the course overview and convert it into a concrete preparation checklist.
slides.transition_to_next: Close by saying that next session will assume Ubuntu is ready and will turn that machine into a working ROS 2 lab environment.
slides.explanation_style: Use FINAL REMINDER as a clear closing checklist, not as a new technical definition.
slides.story_hint: Connect the reminder to the next class: installation readiness protects the limited lab time for actual ROS and manipulator work.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave knowing exactly what must be ready before Session 2 and why it matters.
